1.lecture:
§1 · Position, velocity, acceleration

Kinematics describes motion without yet asking what causes it. Along a straight line, velocity is the rate of change of position, \( v=dx/dt \), and acceleration the rate of change of velocity, \( a=dv/dt \). When the acceleration is constant these integrate to the three equations of motion that solve most rectilinear problems: \( v=v_0+at \), \( x=x_0+v_0t+\tfrac12at^2 \), and \( v^2=v_0^2+2a(x-x_0) \). A car starting from rest at \( a=3\ \text{m/s}^2 \) for \( 5 \) s reaches \( v=3(5)=15 \) m/s having travelled \( x=\tfrac12(3)(5^2)=37.5 \) m — and the third equation checks it, \( v=\sqrt{2(3)(37.5)}=15 \) m/s. These are the workhorses; the rest of the lesson is what changes when the path bends.

§2 · Curved paths: normal and tangential

When a particle follows a curve, velocity stays tangent to the path but acceleration generally does not. The natural split is into a tangential component \( a_t=dv/dt \), which changes the speed, and a normal component \( a_n=\dfrac{v^2}{\rho} \), which changes the direction and always points toward the centre of curvature (here \( \rho \) is the radius of curvature). A car cornering at \( v=20 \) m/s on a curve of radius \( \rho=100 \) m has \( a_n=400/100=4\ \text{m/s}^2 \) inward even at constant speed; if it is also speeding up at \( a_t=2\ \text{m/s}^2 \), the total acceleration is \( a=\sqrt{a_t^2+a_n^2}=\sqrt{20}\approx4.47\ \text{m/s}^2 \). Direction change is itself an acceleration — the fact that makes cornering and rotating machinery demand force.

Key result — normal-tangential acceleration

\[ a_t=\frac{dv}{dt}\ \text{(speed change)},\qquad a_n=\frac{v^2}{\rho}\ \text{(direction change, toward centre)}. \]

§3 · Projectile motion

A projectile is just two rectilinear motions at once: constant velocity horizontally, constant acceleration \( -g \) vertically. Launch speed \( v_0 \) at angle \( \theta \) gives horizontal \( v_0\cos\theta \) and vertical \( v_0\sin\theta \). The range on level ground is \( R=\dfrac{v_0^2\sin2\theta}{g} \) and the peak height \( H=\dfrac{v_0^2\sin^2\theta}{2g} \). For \( v_0=20 \) m/s at \( 30^\circ \): \( R=\dfrac{400\sin60^\circ}{9.81}\approx35.3 \) m and \( H=\dfrac{400(0.25)}{2(9.81)}\approx5.10 \) m. The two directions are solved separately and only share the clock — the single most useful idea in projectile problems.

§4 · The working method

The disciplined routine is: (1) choose a coordinate description that matches the motion — rectilinear for straight-line, normal-tangential for curved paths, polar for radial problems; (2) write velocity and acceleration in that description; (3) for constant acceleration reach for the three equations of motion, otherwise integrate \( a\to v\to x \). Keep speed change (tangential) and direction change (normal) mentally separate on any curved path, because forgetting the \( v^2/\rho \) term is the commonest error in the subject — and it is exactly the term that, multiplied by mass in the next lesson, becomes the centripetal force a road, a bearing, or a turbine blade must supply.
1.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Derivatives and integrals — velocity and acceleration are rates.
  • Vectors (from math) — motion in two dimensions.
  • Right-triangle trigonometry — resolving components.

Toolkit — laws and formulas

QuantityFormulaWhen to use
Velocity / acceleration\( v=\dfrac{dx}{dt},\ a=\dfrac{dv}{dt} \)Definitions.
Constant-a equations\( v=v_0+at,\ x=x_0+v_0t+\tfrac12at^2 \)Rectilinear, constant acceleration.
Velocity–position\( v^2=v_0^2+2a(x-x_0) \)When time is not wanted.
Tangential accel\( a_t=dv/dt \)Change of speed.
Normal accel\( a_n=v^2/\rho \)Change of direction (toward centre).
Projectile range\( R=\dfrac{v_0^2\sin2\theta}{g} \)Level-ground range.

quiz.q:
Rectilinear from rest

Problem. A car starts from rest and accelerates at \( 3\ \text{m/s}^2 \) for \( 5 \) s. Find its final speed and the distance travelled.

Normal and tangential acceleration

Problem. A car rounds a curve of radius \( \rho=100 \) m at \( v=20 \) m/s while speeding up at \( a_t=2\ \text{m/s}^2 \). Find the normal acceleration and the total acceleration.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( a_n=\dfrac{v^2}{\rho}=\dfrac{400}{100}=4\ \text{m/s}^2 \) (toward the centre). The total is the vector sum of perpendicular parts: \( a=\sqrt{a_t^2+a_n^2}=\sqrt{2^2+4^2}=\sqrt{20}\approx4.47\ \text{m/s}^2 \).

quiz.q:
Projectile

Problem. A ball is launched at \( v_0=20 \) m/s at \( 30^\circ \) above the horizontal. Find the range and the maximum height (\( g=9.81\ \text{m/s}^2 \)).
quiz.solution: Solution. Range \( R=\dfrac{v_0^2\sin2\theta}{g}=\dfrac{400\sin60^\circ}{9.81}\approx35.3 \) m. Max height \( H=\dfrac{v_0^2\sin^2\theta}{2g}=\dfrac{400(0.25)}{19.62}\approx5.10 \) m. Horizontal and vertical motions are solved separately, sharing only the time.

quiz.q: For constant acceleration, the velocity after time \(t\) is:
quiz.solution: \( v=v_0+at \) — the integral of constant acceleration.

quiz.q: The normal (centripetal) acceleration on a curved path is:
quiz.solution: \( a_n=v^2/\rho \), directed toward the centre of curvature — it changes direction, not speed.

quiz.q: A car from rest at \( 3\ \text{m/s}^2 \) reaches, after 5 s, a speed of:
The distance in the previous case is:
At \( v=20 \) m/s on a curve of radius \(100\) m, the normal acceleration is:
The tangential acceleration \( a_t \) represents:
quiz.solution: \( a_t=dv/dt \) — the rate at which speed changes along the path.

quiz.q: In projectile motion, the horizontal and vertical motions:
quiz.solution: Horizontal is constant-velocity, vertical is constant-acceleration \(-g\); they share only the clock.

quiz.q: A projectile launched at \( 20 \) m/s and \( 30^\circ \) has a range of about:
quiz.solution: \( R=v_0^2\sin2\theta/g=400\sin60^\circ/9.81\approx35.3 \) m.

1.kuwait:
A road engineer setting the design speed of a highway curve is working the normal-acceleration equation directly. The lateral acceleration a driver feels is \( a_n=v^2/\rho \), and comfort and safety limits cap it — which fixes the minimum radius \( \rho \) for a given speed, or the safe speed for a given radius. Superelevation (banking the road) and the friction budget of the next lesson both exist to supply exactly this \( v^2/\rho \) demand; underestimate it and vehicles slide off the bend.

The same term sizes rotating machinery on the plant floor. A point on a spinning impeller or grinding wheel at radius \(r\) undergoes centripetal acceleration \( a_n=v^2/r=\omega^2 r \), and multiplied by mass this becomes the outward force the material and fasteners must hold. It is why maximum safe speeds are stamped on grinding wheels and why overspeed on a turbine is catastrophic: the direction-changing acceleration, not the speed itself, is what tears components apart.

2.lecture:
§1 · F = ma, applied through a free-body diagram

Newton's second law, \( \sum\mathbf{F}=m\mathbf{a} \), connects the forces on a particle to its acceleration. The whole discipline of using it is the free-body diagram (FBD): isolate the body, draw every force acting on it — weight, normal contact, friction, applied loads, tension — and nothing else, then sum components along convenient axes. On a curved or inclined path the axes are chosen to match the motion, which is why the normal–tangential split of the last lesson matters here. Get the FBD right and the algebra is routine; get it wrong — a missing or invented force — and no amount of algebra recovers. The FBD is the single most important habit in all of mechanics.

§2 · Friction and the incline

On a plane inclined at \( \theta \), the weight \( mg \) resolves into a component \( mg\sin\theta \) driving the block down the slope and a component \( mg\cos\theta \) pressing it into the surface. The surface pushes back with the normal force \( N=mg\cos\theta \), and dry friction opposes sliding up to a limit \( f\le\mu N \). For a \( 10 \) kg block on a \( 30^\circ \) incline: \( N=10(9.81)\cos30^\circ\approx84.96 \) N, driving force \( mg\sin30^\circ=49.05 \) N, and maximum friction \( \mu N=0.2(84.96)\approx16.99 \) N. Since the drive exceeds friction, the block slides, and Newton's law along the slope gives \( a=g(\sin\theta-\mu\cos\theta)=9.81(0.5-0.173)\approx3.21\ \text{m/s}^2 \).

Key relations — incline & friction

\( N=mg\cos\theta \); driving force \( =mg\sin\theta \); friction \( f\le\mu N \). Sliding: \( a=g(\sin\theta-\mu\cos\theta) \).

§3 · Curvilinear motion: the centripetal force

The normal acceleration \( a_n=v^2/\rho \) of the last lesson, multiplied by mass, is a real force requirement: turning a particle of mass \(m\) on a curve of radius \(r\) demands a centripetal force \( F=\dfrac{mv^2}{r} \) directed toward the centre. On a flat road that force can only come from friction, \( \mu mg\ge\dfrac{mv^2}{r} \), which caps the cornering speed at \( v_{\max}=\sqrt{\mu g r} \). For \( \mu=0.7,\ r=50 \) m: \( v_{\max}=\sqrt{0.7(9.81)(50)}\approx18.5 \) m/s (about \(67\) km/h). Exceed it and the friction budget is spent, the required centripetal force cannot be supplied, and the vehicle slides straight on — Newton's law making a hard physical prediction.

§4 · The working method

For any kinetics problem: (1) draw the free-body diagram — every real force, no more; (2) choose axes that match the motion (along/across a slope, normal/tangential on a curve); (3) write \( \sum F=ma \) in each direction and solve. Two checks guard the work: the normal direction usually has no acceleration (so \( \sum F=0 \) there, fixing \(N\)), and units must close to newtons. This routine handles the block, the cornering car, the elevator, and the whole of the following energy and momentum lessons, which are Newton's law integrated over distance and over time respectively.
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Particle kinematics (Lesson 1) — \(a\), and \( a_n=v^2/\rho \).
  • Vector components — resolving forces along axes.
  • Weight \( W=mg \) — the ever-present force.

Toolkit — laws and formulas

QuantityFormulaWhen to use
Newton's 2nd law\( \sum\mathbf{F}=m\mathbf{a} \)Forces ↔ acceleration.
Weight\( W=mg \)Gravity force.
Normal (incline)\( N=mg\cos\theta \)Contact force on a slope.
Dry friction\( f\le\mu N \)Opposes/limits sliding.
Centripetal force\( F=mv^2/r \)Force to turn on a curve.
Max cornering speed\( v_{\max}=\sqrt{\mu g r} \)Friction-limited on a flat curve.

quiz.q:
Block on an incline

Problem. A \(10\) kg block sits on a \(30^\circ\) incline with \( \mu=0.2 \). Find its acceleration down the slope (\( g=9.81 \)).
quiz.solution: Solution. Normal: \( N=mg\cos\theta=84.96 \) N. Along the slope: driving \( mg\sin\theta=49.05 \) N, friction \( \mu N=16.99 \) N opposing. Newton's law: \( ma=mg\sin\theta-\mu N \), so \( a=g(\sin\theta-\mu\cos\theta)=9.81(0.5-0.173)\approx3.21\ \text{m/s}^2 \).

quiz.q:
Cornering speed

Problem. A car corners on a flat road of radius \(50\) m with tyre friction \( \mu=0.7 \). Find the maximum speed before it slides.
quiz.solution: Solution. Friction must supply the centripetal force: \( \mu mg\ge mv^2/r \). The mass cancels: \( v_{\max}=\sqrt{\mu g r}=\sqrt{0.7(9.81)(50)}=\sqrt{343.4}\approx18.5 \) m/s (\(\approx67\) km/h).

quiz.q:
Centripetal force

Problem. Find the centripetal force on a \(1200\) kg car cornering at \(18.5\) m/s on a \(50\) m radius, and confirm it matches the available friction.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( F=\dfrac{mv^2}{r}=\dfrac{1200(18.5)^2}{50}\approx8240 \) N. Available friction \( \mu mg=0.7(1200)(9.81)\approx8240 \) N — they match, confirming the car is exactly at its limit.

quiz.q: Newton's second law states:
quiz.solution: The net force equals mass times acceleration: \( \sum\mathbf{F}=m\mathbf{a} \).

quiz.q: The maximum dry-friction force is:
quiz.solution: \( f\le\mu N \) — the limit is \( \mu \) times the normal force (which is \(mg\) only on a level surface).

quiz.q: On a \(30^\circ\) incline, the component of weight along the slope is:
quiz.solution: The driving component is \( mg\sin\theta \); the pressing (normal) component is \( mg\cos\theta \).

quiz.q: The force needed to turn a mass on a curve of radius \(r\) is:
quiz.solution: The centripetal force \( mv^2/r \) points toward the centre — the outward "centrifugal" force is a reaction felt in a rotating frame, not the real applied force.

quiz.q: The max cornering speed on a flat curve is:
quiz.solution: Setting \( \mu mg=mv^2/r \) and cancelling \(m\) gives \( v_{\max}=\sqrt{\mu g r} \).

quiz.q: In the normal (perpendicular-to-motion) direction on a slope, the acceleration is usually:
quiz.solution: The block does not leave the surface, so there is no normal acceleration; \( \sum F=0 \) perpendicular gives \( N=mg\cos\theta \).

quiz.q: An elevator accelerating upward makes the apparent weight of a passenger:
quiz.solution: The floor must supply \( N=m(g+a) \) to accelerate the passenger up — the felt weight rises.

quiz.q: The maximum cornering speed \( \sqrt{\mu g r} \) is independent of:
quiz.solution: The mass cancels in \( \mu mg=mv^2/r \), so the limit depends only on \( \mu, g, r \) — not on how heavy the vehicle is.

2.kuwait:
A haul-road or ramp designer at a quarry or plant lives on the incline equations. Whether a loaded truck can climb a grade, or will slide on a wet slope, is decided by \( \tan\theta \) against the available friction \( \mu \): once \( \sin\theta \) exceeds \( \mu\cos\theta \) the vehicle accelerates downhill uncontrollably. The same balance sets how much a conveyor can incline before material slips back, and how steep a stored pile can stand — the angle of repose is just the slope at which \( \tan\theta=\mu \).

The cornering limit \( v_{\max}=\sqrt{\mu gr} \) governs every guided vehicle on site, from forklifts to AGVs to rail curves. Because the mass cancels, the safe speed depends only on the radius and the friction, which is why speed limits tighten on tight bends and why oil or dust on the floor — cutting \( \mu \) — is a genuine hazard: it lowers \( v_{\max} \) without changing anything the driver can see.

3.lecture:
§1 · Work: force acting through distance

The work done by a constant force is \( W=Fd\cos\theta \) — the force times the distance times the cosine of the angle between them, so only the component of force along the motion counts. A force perpendicular to the path (a normal force, the tension in a turning string) does no work at all. This is the dot product of the vector lessons, integrated along a path. Work is measured in joules, and it is the currency in which the whole energy method is denominated: instead of tracking forces and accelerations moment by moment, we track how much work is done and where it goes.

§2 · Kinetic energy and the work–energy theorem

A moving particle carries kinetic energy \( KE=\tfrac12mv^2 \), and the work–energy theorem states that the net work done on it equals its change in kinetic energy: \( W_{\text{net}}=\Delta KE \). This is Newton's law integrated over distance, and it is an accounting identity — energy in equals energy stored. A \( 100 \) N force pushing a \( 20 \) kg block from rest over \( 10 \) m (frictionless) does \( W=1000 \) J, so \( \tfrac12(20)v^2=1000 \Rightarrow v=10 \) m/s. The power of the method is that it never asks about the acceleration or the time — only the endpoints — which is exactly what makes it the fast route through problems where forces vary along the way.

Key identity — work–energy

\[ W_{\text{net}}=\Delta KE=\tfrac12mv_2^2-\tfrac12mv_1^2. \]

§3 · Potential energy and conservation

Some forces store the work done against them as potential energy, recoverable later: gravity stores \( PE=mgh \), a spring stores \( \tfrac12kx^2 \). When only such conservative forces act, the sum \( KE+PE \) is constant — conservation of energy. A mass dropped from height \( h \) converts all its \( mgh \) into \( \tfrac12mv^2 \), giving \( v=\sqrt{2gh} \); from \( h=5 \) m, \( v=\sqrt{2(9.81)(5)}\approx9.9 \) m/s, independent of the mass. A spring of stiffness \( k=2000 \) N/m compressed \( x=0.1 \) m stores \( \tfrac12(2000)(0.1)^2=10 \) J, enough to launch a \( 0.5 \) kg mass to \( v=\sqrt{2(10)/0.5}\approx6.3 \) m/s. Friction, by contrast, is non-conservative — it drains mechanical energy into heat, and its work is where the "lost" energy goes.

§4 · Power and the working method

Power is the rate of doing work, \( P=\dfrac{dW}{dt}=Fv \) — the number that sizes motors and engines. A \( 500 \) N pull at \( 2 \) m/s delivers \( 1000 \) W. The energy method's routine is: (1) identify the states (start and end) and the energies present at each — kinetic, gravitational, spring; (2) account for any work added by applied forces or drained by friction; (3) set energy-in equal to energy-out and solve. Choose energy over \( F=ma \) whenever the question links speed to position and the time in between does not matter — falling bodies, spring launches, roller-coaster speeds — because it collapses a differential equation into one algebraic balance.
3.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Newton's second law (Lesson 2) — energy is it, integrated over distance.
  • The dot product — work counts the along-path force component.
  • Springs \( F=kx \) — the source of elastic energy.

Toolkit — laws and formulas

QuantityFormulaWhen to use
Work\( W=Fd\cos\theta \)Force through distance.
Kinetic energy\( KE=\tfrac12mv^2 \)Energy of motion.
Work–energy theorem\( W_{\text{net}}=\Delta KE \)Link work to speed change.
Gravitational PE\( PE=mgh \)Height energy.
Spring PE\( \tfrac12kx^2 \)Elastic energy.
Conservation\( KE+PE=\text{const} \)No friction; \( v=\sqrt{2gh} \).
Power\( P=Fv \)Rate of work; motor sizing.

quiz.q:
Work–energy theorem

Problem. A \(100\) N force pushes a \(20\) kg block from rest over \(10\) m on a frictionless floor. Find the work done and the final speed.
quiz.solution: Solution. Work \( W=Fd=100(10)=1000 \) J. By the work–energy theorem \( W=\tfrac12mv^2 \), so \( \tfrac12(20)v^2=1000\Rightarrow v^2=100\Rightarrow v=10 \) m/s.

quiz.q:
Falling body

Problem. A mass is released from rest and falls \(h=5\) m. Find its speed at the bottom (\( g=9.81 \)).
quiz.solution: Solution. Conservation: \( mgh=\tfrac12mv^2 \), so \( v=\sqrt{2gh}=\sqrt{2(9.81)(5)}=\sqrt{98.1}\approx9.9 \) m/s — independent of the mass.

quiz.q:
Spring launch

Problem. A spring of stiffness \(k=2000\) N/m is compressed \(x=0.1\) m. Find the stored energy and the launch speed of a \(0.5\) kg mass.
quiz.solution: Solution. Stored energy \( \tfrac12kx^2=\tfrac12(2000)(0.1)^2=10 \) J. All of it becomes kinetic: \( \tfrac12(0.5)v^2=10\Rightarrow v^2=40\Rightarrow v\approx6.3 \) m/s.

quiz.q: The work done by a constant force is:
quiz.solution: Only the component of force along the motion does work: \( W=Fd\cos\theta \).

quiz.q: Kinetic energy is:
quiz.solution: \( KE=\tfrac12mv^2 \).

quiz.q: The work–energy theorem states \( W_{\text{net}}= \):
quiz.solution: Net work equals the change in kinetic energy.

quiz.q: A mass falling freely through height \(h\) reaches speed:
quiz.solution: \( mgh=\tfrac12mv^2\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{2gh} \).

quiz.q: A force perpendicular to the motion does work equal to:
quiz.solution: \( \cos90^\circ=0 \), so a perpendicular force (e.g. a normal force) does no work.

quiz.q: The energy stored in a spring compressed \(x\) is:
quiz.solution: Spring potential energy is \( \tfrac12kx^2 \).

quiz.q: Power delivered by a force \(F\) at speed \(v\) is:
quiz.solution: Power is the rate of doing work: \( P=Fv \).

quiz.q: Friction is a non-conservative force because it:
quiz.solution: Friction's work is lost from the mechanical balance as heat — it cannot be recovered, unlike spring or gravitational energy.

3.kuwait:
An engineer sizing a hoist or conveyor motor works straight from power, \( P=Fv \). Lifting a load at a steady rate needs at least the power to raise its weight, \( P=mg\,v_{\text{lift}} \), plus friction losses; picking a motor below that figure means it stalls under load. The energy view also sizes braking: stopping a moving mass means absorbing its kinetic energy \( \tfrac12mv^2 \), which is why a brake or a buffer at the end of a travel is rated in joules, and why doubling the speed quadruples the braking energy a crane or lift must dissipate.

Conservation of energy is the quick estimator on the plant floor. The speed a dropped tool or a falling load reaches is \( v=\sqrt{2gh} \) regardless of its mass — which is why fall-arrest and drop-zone calculations depend only on height, and why a modest drop can still produce a dangerous impact. Reading a hazard as an energy \( mgh \) to be caught, rather than a force to be resisted, is how guarding and containment are actually specified.

4.lecture:
§1 · Impulse changes momentum

Integrate Newton's law over time instead of distance and you get the impulse–momentum principle: the impulse \( J=\int F\,dt \) of a force equals the change in momentum \( p=mv \) it produces, \( J=\Delta p=m\Delta v \). For a constant force this is simply \( J=F\,\Delta t \). A \( 200 \) N force acting for \( 0.1 \) s delivers \( J=20 \) N·s, which brings a \( 2 \) kg mass from rest to \( \Delta v=J/m=10 \) m/s. This is the natural tool for short, hard interactions — a hammer blow, a kick, a coupling impact — where the force is large, brief, and hard to measure, but its time-integral is exactly what changes the motion.

§2 · Conservation of momentum

When no external impulse acts on a system, its total momentum is conserved — the internal forces between colliding bodies are equal and opposite, so they cancel in the sum. For two bodies, \( m_1v_1+m_2v_2=m_1v_1'+m_2v_2' \) through the collision. This holds whether the collision is bouncy or sticky, which is what makes it so powerful: it constrains the after-state without any knowledge of the messy forces during contact. It is the principle behind recoil, rocket thrust, and every impact calculation in the lesson.

Key principles — impulse & momentum

\[ J=\int F\,dt=\Delta p=m\Delta v;\qquad \sum m_iv_i=\sum m_iv_i'\ \text{(no external impulse)}. \]

§3 · Impact and the coefficient of restitution

Momentum conservation gives one equation, but a collision of two bodies has two unknown final velocities, so a second relation is needed: the coefficient of restitution \( e=\dfrac{\text{separation speed}}{\text{approach speed}}=\dfrac{v_2'-v_1'}{v_1-v_2} \), a number between \(0\) and \(1\) set by the materials. A perfectly elastic impact has \( e=1 \) and conserves kinetic energy; a perfectly plastic one has \( e=0 \) and the bodies stick. Take a \( 2 \) kg body at \( 3 \) m/s striking a stationary \( 1 \) kg body and sticking (\( e=0 \)): momentum gives \( v'=\dfrac{2(3)}{3}=2 \) m/s. The kinetic energy falls from \( \tfrac12(2)(3^2)=9 \) J to \( \tfrac12(3)(2^2)=6 \) J — \( 3 \) J lost to deformation and heat. Energy is not conserved in impact unless \( e=1 \).

§4 · The working method

For any impact: (1) draw the before and after states with velocities and signs on one axis; (2) write conservation of momentum \( \sum m_iv_i=\sum m_iv_i' \); (3) add the restitution relation \( e=(v_2'-v_1')/(v_1-v_2) \) as the second equation, and solve the pair. For a stick (\(e=0\)) the two bodies share a common final velocity and one equation suffices. Never assume kinetic energy is conserved — check \(e\) first, because treating a real, lossy impact as elastic is the classic error. Impulse–momentum over time and work–energy over distance are the two integrals of Newton's law, and together they answer almost every particle problem without solving a differential equation.
4.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Newton's second law (Lesson 2) — impulse is it, integrated over time.
  • Kinetic energy (Lesson 3) — to see what impact loses.
  • Signed one-dimensional velocities — direction bookkeeping.

Toolkit — laws and formulas

QuantityFormulaWhen to use
Momentum\( p=mv \)Quantity of motion.
Impulse\( J=\int F\,dt=F\Delta t \)Effect of a force over time.
Impulse–momentum\( J=\Delta p \)Short, hard interactions.
Conservation\( \sum m_iv_i=\sum m_iv_i' \)No external impulse.
Restitution\( e=\dfrac{v_2'-v_1'}{v_1-v_2} \)Second impact equation.
Elastic / plastic\( e=1 \) / \( e=0 \)KE conserved / bodies stick.

quiz.q:
Impulse on a mass

Problem. A constant \(200\) N force acts on a \(2\) kg mass (initially at rest) for \(0.1\) s. Find the impulse and the final speed.
quiz.solution: Solution. Impulse \( J=F\Delta t=200(0.1)=20 \) N·s. By impulse–momentum \( J=m\Delta v \), so \( \Delta v=20/2=10 \) m/s. Starting from rest, the final speed is \(10\) m/s.

quiz.q:
Perfectly plastic collision

Problem. A \(2\) kg body moving at \(3\) m/s strikes a stationary \(1\) kg body and they stick together. Find the common velocity and the kinetic energy lost.
quiz.solution: Solution. Momentum: \( 2(3)+1(0)=(2+1)v'\Rightarrow v'=\dfrac{6}{3}=2 \) m/s. \( KE \) before \( =\tfrac12(2)(3^2)=9 \) J; after \( =\tfrac12(3)(2^2)=6 \) J. Lost \( =9-6=3 \) J (to deformation and heat).

quiz.q:
Restitution

Problem. Two equal masses collide, one at \(4\) m/s striking the other at rest, with \( e=0.5 \). Find the two final velocities.
quiz.solution: Solution. Momentum (equal masses): \( v_1'+v_2'=4 \). Restitution: \( v_2'-v_1'=e(v_1-v_2)=0.5(4)=2 \). Solving: \( v_2'=3 \) m/s, \( v_1'=1 \) m/s. (Momentum check: \(1+3=4\) ✓.)

quiz.q: Impulse equals:
quiz.solution: \( J=\int F\,dt=\Delta p \) — impulse is the change in momentum.

quiz.q: For a constant force, impulse is:
quiz.solution: \( J=F\Delta t \) for a constant force.

quiz.q: Total momentum is conserved when:
quiz.solution: Internal collision forces cancel, so momentum is conserved whenever no external impulse acts — elastic or not.

quiz.q: The coefficient of restitution is:
A perfectly elastic collision has:
quiz.solution: \( e=1 \) means kinetic energy is fully recovered — an elastic impact.

quiz.q: A perfectly plastic collision has:
quiz.solution: \( e=0 \): the separation speed is zero, so the bodies move off together.

quiz.q: In the 2 kg + 1 kg sticking collision above, kinetic energy:
quiz.solution: \( 9\) J before, \(6\) J after — \(3\) J is lost to the impact, since \(e=0\).

quiz.q: To deliver a given impulse with a smaller peak force, you should:
quiz.solution: Since \( J=F\Delta t \) is fixed by the momentum change, a longer \( \Delta t \) means a lower \(F\) — the principle behind crumple zones and cushioning.

4.kuwait:
The impulse relation \( J=F\Delta t \) is the physics of every cushion, crumple zone, and pipe-hanger snubber on site. The momentum a moving mass must lose in a stop is fixed, so the only way to cut the peak force is to stretch the time of the stop: a longer \( \Delta t \) means a smaller \(F\). That is why buffers, shock mounts, and packaging exist, and why a hammer blow — a very short \( \Delta t \) — produces an enormous force from a modest swing, the tool a fitter uses to seat a pin that steady pressure cannot.

Momentum conservation sizes the reaction forces of fluid machinery. A jet of water striking a plate, or the change of direction inside a pump volute or a pipe bend, changes the fluid's momentum, and by Newton's third law the structure feels the equal and opposite force. Anchor blocks on large pipe bends and the thrust rating of a nozzle are momentum calculations — \( F=\dot m\,\Delta v \) — turned into bolts and concrete.
